problem text: Consider the equation sin(x) + x - 1 = 0. Determine whether there is at least one real solution, justify why such a solution exists, and approximate its value.
problem solution: Let $$f(x)=\sin(x)+x-1$$. Observe that $$f(x)$$ is continuous for all real $$x$$ since both $$\sin(x)$$ and $$x$$ are continuous. First, evaluate $$f(0)=\sin(0)+0-1=-1<0$$ and $$f(1)=\sin(1)+1-1=\sin(1)>0$$, since $$\sin(1)\approx0.84>0$$. Because $$f(0)<0$$ and $$f(1)>0$$, by the Intermediate Value Theorem, there must be at least one root in the interval $$(0,1)$$. Moreover, the derivative is $$f'(x)=\cos(x)+1$$. Since $$-1\le\cos(x)\le1$$ for all real $$x$$, we have $$0\le\cos(x)+1\le2\!.\!$$ Hence $$f'(x)\ge0$$ for all $$x$$, implying that $$f(x)$$ is non-decreasing. Therefore, the zero in $$(0,1)$$ is the unique real solution. A numerical approximation yields $$x\approx0.51\!.\!$$
problem hint: Use the Intermediate Value Theorem on sin(x) + x - 1, checking its sign at convenient points.
